short_excerpt: Rules of Hooks
text: Rules of Hooks

short_excerpt: You are probably here because you got the following error message:
text: You are probably here because you got the following error message:

short_excerpt: There are three common reasons you might be seeing it:
text: There are three common reasons you might be seeing it:

short_excerpt: You might be breaking the Rules of Hooks .
text: You might be breaking the Rules of Hooks .

short_excerpt: You might have mismatching versions of React and React DOM.
text: You might have mismatching versions of React and React DOM.

short_excerpt: You might have more than one copy of React in the same app.
text: You might have more than one copy of React in the same app.

short_excerpt: Let’s look at each of these cases.
text: Let’s look at each of these cases.

short_excerpt: Breaking Rules of Hooks
text: Breaking Rules of Hooks

short_excerpt: Functions whose names start with use are called Hooks in React.
text: Functions whose names start with use are called Hooks in React.

short_excerpt: Don’t call Hooks inside loops, conditions, or nested functions. Instead, always use Hooks at the top level of your React function, before any early returns. You can only call Hooks while React is rendering a function component:
text: Don’t call Hooks inside loops, conditions, or nested functions. Instead, always use Hooks at the top level of your React function, before any early returns. You can only call Hooks while React is rendering a function component:

short_excerpt: ✅ Call them at the top level in the body of a function component .
text: ✅ Call them at the top level in the body of a function component .

short_excerpt: ✅ Call them at the top level in the body of a custom Hook .
text: ✅ Call them at the top level in the body of a custom Hook .

short_excerpt: function Counter ( ) { // ✅ Good: top-level in a function component const [ count , setCount ] = useState ( 0 ) ; // ... } function useWindowWidth ( ) { // ✅ Good: top-level in a custom Hook const [ width , setWidth ] = useState ( window . innerWidth ) ; // ... }
text: function Counter ( ) { // ✅ Good: top-level in a function component const [ count , setCount ] = useState ( 0 ) ; // ... } function useWindowWidth ( ) { // ✅ Good: top-level in a custom Hook const [ width , setWidth ] = useState ( window . innerWidth ) ; // ... }

short_excerpt: It’s not supported to call Hooks (functions starting with use ) in any other cases, for example:
text: It’s not supported to call Hooks (functions starting with use ) in any other cases, for example:

short_excerpt: 🔴 Do not call Hooks inside conditions or loops.
text: 🔴 Do not call Hooks inside conditions or loops.

short_excerpt: 🔴 Do not call Hooks after a conditional return statement.
text: 🔴 Do not call Hooks after a conditional return statement.

short_excerpt: 🔴 Do not call Hooks in event handlers.
text: 🔴 Do not call Hooks in event handlers.

short_excerpt: 🔴 Do not call Hooks in class components.
text: 🔴 Do not call Hooks in class components.

short_excerpt: 🔴 Do not call Hooks inside functions passed to useMemo , useReducer , or useEffect .
text: 🔴 Do not call Hooks inside functions passed to useMemo , useReducer , or useEffect .

short_excerpt: If you break these rules, you might see this error.
text: If you break these rules, you might see this error.

short_excerpt: function Bad ( { cond } ) { if ( cond ) { // 🔴 Bad: inside a condition (to fix, move it outside!) const theme = useContext ( ThemeContext ) ; } // ... } function Bad ( ) { for ( let i = 0 ; i < 10 ; i ++ ) { // 🔴 Bad: inside a loop (to fix, move it outside!) const theme = useContext ( ThemeContext ) ; } // ... } function Bad ( { cond } ) { if ( cond ) { return ; } // 🔴 Bad: after a conditional return (to fix, move it before the return!) const theme = useContext ( ThemeContext ) ; // ... } function Bad ( ) { function handleClick ( ) { // 🔴 Bad: inside an event handler (to fix, move it outside!)
text: function Bad ( { cond } ) { if ( cond ) { // 🔴 Bad: inside a condition (to fix, move it outside!) const theme = useContext ( ThemeContext ) ; } // ... } function Bad ( ) { for ( let i = 0 ; i < 10 ; i ++ ) { // 🔴 Bad: inside a loop (to fix, move it outside!) const theme = useContext ( ThemeContext ) ; } // ... } function Bad ( { cond } ) { if ( cond ) { return ; } // 🔴 Bad: after a conditional return (to fix, move it before the return!) const theme = useContext ( ThemeContext ) ; // ... } function Bad ( ) { function handleClick ( ) { // 🔴 Bad: inside an event handler (to fix, move it outside!) const theme = useContext ( ThemeContext ) ; } // ... } function Bad ( ) { const style = useMemo ( ( ) => { // 🔴 Bad: inside useMemo (to fix, move it outside!) const theme = useContext ( ThemeContext ) ; return createStyle ( theme ) ; } ) ; // ... } class Bad extends React . Component { render ( ) { // 🔴 Bad: inside a class component (to fix, write a function component instead of a class!) useEffect ( ( ) => { } ) // ... } }

short_excerpt: You can use the eslint-plugin-react-hooks plugin to catch these mistakes.
text: You can use the eslint-plugin-react-hooks plugin to catch these mistakes.

short_excerpt: eslint-plugin-react-hooks
text: eslint-plugin-react-hooks

short_excerpt: Note
text: Note

short_excerpt: Custom Hooks may call other Hooks (that’s their whole purpose). This works because custom Hooks are also supposed to only be called while a function component is rendering.
text: Custom Hooks may call other Hooks (that’s their whole purpose). This works because custom Hooks are also supposed to only be called while a function component is rendering.

short_excerpt: Mismatching Versions of React and React DOM
text: Mismatching Versions of React and React DOM

short_excerpt: You might be using a version of react-dom (< 16.8.0) or react-native (< 0.59) that doesn’t yet support Hooks. You can run npm ls react-dom or npm ls react-native in your application folder to check which version you’re using. If you find more than one of them, this might also create problems (more on that below).
text: You might be using a version of react-dom (< 16.8.0) or react-native (< 0.59) that doesn’t yet support Hooks. You can run npm ls react-dom or npm ls react-native in your application folder to check which version you’re using. If you find more than one of them, this might also create problems (more on that below).

short_excerpt: Duplicate React
text: Duplicate React

short_excerpt: In order for Hooks to work, the react import from your application code needs to resolve to the same module as the react import from inside the react-dom package.
text: In order for Hooks to work, the react import from your application code needs to resolve to the same module as the react import from inside the react-dom package.

short_excerpt: If these react imports resolve to two different exports objects, you will see this warning. This may happen if you accidentally end up with two copies of the react package.
text: If these react imports resolve to two different exports objects, you will see this warning. This may happen if you accidentally end up with two copies of the react package.

short_excerpt: If you use Node for package management, you can run this check in your project folder:
text: If you use Node for package management, you can run this check in your project folder:

short_excerpt: npm ls react
text: npm ls react

short_excerpt: If you see more than one React, you’ll need to figure out why this happens and fix your dependency tree. For example, maybe a library you’re using incorrectly specifies react as a dependency (rather than a peer dependency). Until that library is fixed, Yarn resolutions is one possible workaround.
text: If you see more than one React, you’ll need to figure out why this happens and fix your dependency tree. For example, maybe a library you’re using incorrectly specifies react as a dependency (rather than a peer dependency). Until that library is fixed, Yarn resolutions is one possible workaround.

short_excerpt: You can also try to debug this problem by adding some logs and restarting your development server:
text: You can also try to debug this problem by adding some logs and restarting your development server:

short_excerpt: // Add this in node_modules/react-dom/index.js window . React1 = require ( 'react' ) ; // Add this in your component file require ( 'react-dom' ) ; window . React2 = require ( 'react' ) ; console . log ( window . React1 === window . React2 ) ;
text: // Add this in node_modules/react-dom/index.js window . React1 = require ( 'react' ) ; // Add this in your component file require ( 'react-dom' ) ; window . React2 = require ( 'react' ) ; console . log ( window . React1 === window . React2 ) ;

short_excerpt: If it prints false then you might have two Reacts and need to figure out why that happened. This issue includes some common reasons encountered by the community.
text: If it prints false then you might have two Reacts and need to figure out why that happened. This issue includes some common reasons encountered by the community.

short_excerpt: This problem can also come up when you use npm link or an equivalent. In that case, your bundler might “see” two Reacts — one in application folder and one in your library folder. Assuming myapp and mylib are sibling folders, one possible fix is to run npm link ../myapp/node_modules/react from mylib . This should make the library use the application’s React copy.
text: This problem can also come up when you use npm link or an equivalent. In that case, your bundler might “see” two Reacts — one in application folder and one in your library folder. Assuming myapp and mylib are sibling folders, one possible fix is to run npm link ../myapp/node_modules/react from mylib . This should make the library use the application’s React copy.

short_excerpt: In general, React supports using multiple independent copies on one page (for example, if an app and a third-party widget both use it). It only breaks if require('react') resolves differently between the component and the react-dom copy it was rendered with.
text: In general, React supports using multiple independent copies on one page (for example, if an app and a third-party widget both use it). It only breaks if require('react') resolves differently between the component and the react-dom copy it was rendered with.

short_excerpt: Other Causes
text: Other Causes

short_excerpt: If none of this worked, please comment in this issue and we’ll try to help. Try to create a small reproducing example — you might discover the problem as you’re doing it.
text: If none of this worked, please comment in this issue and we’ll try to help. Try to create a small reproducing example — you might discover the problem as you’re doing it.
